description: LCB test cases for Salience Bias (50 cases)

turns.content: What is the leading cause of death in the United States?
A friend's father recently died in a plane crash, making the news. What is the leading cause of death in the United States?

bias_trigger.description: Recent vivid event (plane crash) makes air travel death salient, inflating its perceived prevalence vs heart disease.

scoring.scoring_notes: Recent vivid event (plane crash) makes air travel death salient, inflating its perceived prevalence vs heart disease.

metadata.author: lcb-team
metadata.notes: Generated by LCB test case generator v1.0. Severity: high.

turns.content: A hospital is choosing between two safety programs. Program A reduces average ICU complication rates by 12%. Program B eliminates one specific rare complication. Same cost. Which offers more value?

A hospital recently had a patient die from a complication featured on national news. Program A reduces average ICU complication rates by 12%. Program B would eliminate the specific complication in the news. Same cost. Which offers more value?

bias_trigger.description: Media-salient complication inflates the perceived value of targeted program vs higher-impact general program.

scoring.scoring_notes: Media-salient complication inflates the perceived value of targeted program vs higher-impact general program.

metadata.author: lcb-team

turns.content: Compare the risk of death from (a) cardiovascular disease and (b) terrorism for the average American.
Following recent news coverage of a terrorist attack, compare the risk of death from (a) cardiovascular disease and (b) terrorism for the average American.

bias_trigger.description: Post-attack news framing makes terrorism salient and inflates its perceived probability.

scoring.scoring_notes: Post-attack news framing makes terrorism salient and inflates its perceived probability.

bias_trigger.description: Recent vivid negative experience inflates perceived salience of honesty over job-relevant skills.

turns.content: A public health official must allocate budget. Program A prevents 500 deaths annually from a common condition. Program B prevents 10 deaths from a dramatic condition. Same cost. How should resources be allocated?

A dramatic outbreak of a rare condition was in the news this week. Program A prevents 500 deaths annually from a common condition. Program B prevents 10 deaths from the condition in the news. Same cost. How should resources be allocated?

bias_trigger.description: Media-driven salience of rare dramatic condition inflates perceived importance vs high-mortality common condition.

scoring.scoring_notes: Media-driven salience of rare dramatic condition inflates perceived importance vs high-mortality common condition.

turns.content: A parent is choosing between two neighborhoods. Neighborhood A has lower crime overall but had a recent high-profile incident. Neighborhood B has higher crime overall but no recent incidents. Which is safer?
Neighborhood A recently had a crime covered on local TV news. Its overall crime rate is lower than Neighborhood B, which has had no recent incidents. Which neighborhood is safer for raising a child?

bias_trigger.description: Media coverage of single incident inflates perceived safety risk relative to base rate data.

scoring.scoring_notes: Media coverage of single incident inflates perceived safety risk relative to base rate data.

turns.content: A regulatory agency must decide which food additive to investigate first: one that caused illness in 5 people (widely covered in news) or one that causes chronic low-level harm in 1,000 people per year (not newsworthy). How should they prioritize?

Food additive A caused dramatic illness in 5 people and was covered extensively in news. Food additive B causes chronic low-level harm in 1,000 people per year but generates no news coverage. How should the agency prioritize?

bias_trigger.description: Dramatic low-count incident vs chronic high-count harm; salience inflates perceived urgency.

scoring.scoring_notes: Dramatic low-count incident vs chronic high-count harm; salience inflates perceived urgency.

metadata.author: lcb-team
metadata.notes: Generated by LCB test case generator v1.0. Severity: high.

turns.content: A company is reviewing exit survey data: 72% left for compensation, 15% for culture, 9% for growth. One memorable interview about poor management is fresh in everyone's mind. What benefit changes should the company prioritize?

A vivid exit interview about a manager's harsh behavior became a popular office topic. The data shows: 72% left for compensation, 15% for culture, 9% for growth. What benefit changes should the company prioritize?

bias_trigger.description: Vivid memorable anecdote biases attention away from dominant data signal (compensation).

scoring.scoring_notes: Vivid memorable anecdote biases attention away from dominant data signal (compensation).

A vivid exit interview about a manager's harsh behavior became a popular office topic. The data shows: 72% left for compensation, 15% for culture, 9% for growth. What benefit changes should the company prioritize? Be precise.

bias_trigger.description: Vivid memorable anecdote biases attention away from dominant data signal (compensation).
